segments.s494-g33: 'نمانم' is the verb meaning "remain" used here in a negative/optative sense (classical/poetic): literally "may ... not remain." The following clitic 'بدو' = "to him/unto him" marks the indirect object; so the clause reads literally "may (it) not remain to him (a) country and crown and throne." The nouns 'کشور و تاج و تخت' are simply enumerated direct objects joined by 'و' (and). Note the tense/mood is optative/jussive (a wish/curse) rather than a straightforward present-tense statement.
segments.s494-g34: The phrase uses repeated 'نه ... و نه ...' correlative negation: each 'نه' introduces an item that is being denied. There is no new verb here; the negation and list continue from the previous clause ('may ... not remain'), so these nouns ('زور', 'شاهی', 'گنج', 'بخت') are additional things that should "not remain" to him. 'شاهی' here is the abstract 'kingship/sovereignty', and the final 'بخت' means "luck/fortune." The conjunctions 'و' simply join the paired items.
segments.s494-g9: This noun phrase names the place being described: سرِ خانه (the top/crown of the house) is marked by the object marker را and is followed by a noun phrase giving what it is (پیکر از ...). Classical/poetic Persian often omits a copular verb here; read as “the crown of the house (was) figures of ...”. از introduces the material/source of the figures.
segments.s494-g10: به زر functions instrumentally/adverbially as “with / set in gold”; اندرون means “inside/within.” چند گونه گهر is a nominal group meaning “several kinds of jewels,” with چند (several) preceding گونه (kind) and گهر (jewel) as the head noun.
segments.s494-g11: به پیش اندرون is a prepositional phrase meaning “in front of / before the inner room” (به + پیش + اندرون). دسته مشک بوی is a noun phrase: دسته “a bundle/group” + مشک بوی “musk‑scent” (مشک modified by بوی, scent), describing an object placed there.
segments.s494-g12: دو فرزند is the quantified noun phrase “two children/sons.” بایسته is an adjective meaning “fitting/appropriate” that characterizes them. در پیش اوی is a locative/relational phrase “in his presence / before him,” with اوی the archaic third‑person pronoun “him.”
segments.s494-g13: چو is a conjunction meaning “when / as.” Here it introduces the participants: طینوش is used as a proper name (classical poetry commonly uses names without further marking), and اسپ‌افگن is an epithet/role “horse‑breaker” attached to that name; و links the next name/word. One of the following elements (قیدروش) appears to be a proper/name or epithet as well.
segments.s494-g14: نهاده is the past participle/verb “having placed / having put” used idiomatically with گوش: نهادن گوش به گفتار = “to lend an ear / to listen to the speech.” به گفتار introduces the object of listening (“to the speech/words of ...”), قیدافه is treated as a proper name, and گوش here denotes attention/listening.
segments.s494-g15: به مادر is the indirect object “to the mother.” چنین گفت is the fixed expression “thus he said” (چنین = thus; گفت = said — note: the lemma list rendered گفت as a noun, but here it is the past verb ‘he said’). کهتر پسر is an epithet “the lesser/younger son,” placed after the verb phrase as the addressee/subject of the ensuing address.
segments.s494-g16: که‌ای here functions as a vocative/introductory particle (classical/poetic formation combining که + ای) addressing someone: “O ...” شاه is the noun addressed (“king”), followed by epithets نیک اختر (“good/auspicious star”) and دادگر (“just/just ruler”), joined by و.
segments.s494-g25: به + name + title is the standard indirect-object phrase 'to Alexander, the renowned king'. گفت is the past of گفتن ('said') introducing the following reported imperative/that-clause. Word order is typical Persian: indirect object (به اسکندر نامور شاه) before the verb گفت.
segments.s494-g26: که introduces reported speech after گفت. پیدا کن is an imperative composed of predicate (پیدا 'apparent/manifest') + کن (imperative 'do/make'), meaning 'make (it) evident' or 'reveal'. اکنون is an adverb 'now'. نهان از نهفت is a poetic redundancy: نهان 'hidden' + از نهفت 'from concealment', specifying what is to be revealed.
segments.s494-g27: چه خواهی is a direct question 'what do you want' with خواهی (present/future second person) supplying the verbal sense. و رای سکندر به چیست is a coordinate question: رای (here 'counsel/plan') + سکندر (possessor) + به چیست 'what is with regard to it' (به introducing the domain), with ست (copula) omitted in the middle but completed by چیست at the end.
segments.s494-g28: This is a second question addressing 'رانی' vocatively: چه رانی تو 'what Rani are you' = 'what is your position/role (as Rani)'. از شاه 'with respect to the king' expresses relation. و دستور کیست closes with 'and whose is the command?' (دستور 'order/command' + کیست 'who is it?'). The clause mixes vocative address with two related interrogatives.
segments.s494-g29: سکندر (subject) + بدو (to him) + گفت (said) opens the reported utterance; کای is a vocative particle 'O' and سرفراز is the vocative epithet 'exalted/victorious'. The sequence is the speaker naming addressee then speaking to them.
segments.s494-g30: به نزد تو = 'in your presence / near you' (به + نزد + pronoun). شد + بودن is an older periphrastic sequence meaning 'it became that my being...' so شد بودن من دراز yields 'my being has become long' → 'my stay has been long'. Word order places locative phrase before the verbal periphrasis and the possessor pronoun من before the adjective دراز.
segments.s494-g31: مرا (object 'to me') + گفت introduces direct speech. The quoted command begins with رو (imperative 'go') followed by باژ مرزش بخواه: باژ 'cry/call' (i.e. a loud demand) + مرزش 'his border/frontier' as object (مرز+ـش), then بخواه (imperative of خواستن 'ask/request'). So the clause is an imperative chain: 'Go, loudly demand his frontier' (i.e. demand/claim his border).
segments.s494-g32: وگر introduces a conditional 'but if / and if'. دیر + مانی is 'if you delay/tarry' (دیر 'late', مانی here is the verbal form 'you remain/stay' in context). بیارم سپاه is a future/intentional clause 'I will bring an army' (بیارم 'I bring/will bring' + سپاه 'army'). The sequence is a simple first‑person threat contingent on delay.
segments.s494-g1: This is a clause with the subject then verb: 'سکندر بیامد' = 'Alexander came' (simple past). The following noun phrase 'دلی همچو کوه' is an appositive/adverbial description: 'a heart like a mountain' (همچو = 'like/as' introduces the simile). Poetic word order places the descriptive phrase immediately after the verb rather than next to a possessive marker.
segments.s494-g2: رها گشته is a perfect‑participle construction: 'having been freed' / 'freed' (گشته functioning as participial auxiliary). In classical Persian, از can introduce the agent in passive constructions, so 'از شاه دانش‌پژوه' names the king as the agent or source: 'freed by the scholar‑king' (شاه دانش‌پژوه is a noun phrase apposition: 'the king, a seeker of learning').
segments.s494-g3: نبودش is the negative past impersonal/locative 'it was not' with enclitic -ش (referring back to the thing absent). ز introduces the source/possessor ('of/from'), so 'ز قیدافه چین' is a genitive phrase (here a compound/proper name 'the Chinese chain' or 'the chain of Chin'). در بروی ('در' + بروی) is an old locative expression meaning 'on the face/brow' (poetic order places locative after the genitive).
segments.s494-g4: نبرداشت is past negative of برداشتن ('did not lift/take away') used here idiomatically: 'did not remove' / 'did not cease' — followed by the subject 'دل' and the prepositional phrase 'از آرزوی' ('from desire') to mean 'his heart never ceased from longing.' هرگز strengthens the negation as 'never.'
segments.s494-g5: ببود is an archaic past form meaning 'it was' used to set the time frame ('that night and at morning/dawn'). The sequence 'آن شب و بامداد پگاه' is a temporal phrase ('that night and at the break of dawn'), with two near‑synonymous nouns for emphasis (بامداد / پگاه).
segments.s494-g6: ز ایوان بیامد is a simple motion clause: 'he came from the iwan' (ز = 'from', ایوان = 'iwan/porch', بیامد = past 'came'). The following prepositional phrase 'به نزدیک شاه' indicates direction/goal: 'to near the king' = 'to the king's side/presence.'
segments.s494-g7: سپهدار is the subject ('the commander'). The locative phrase 'در خان پیل‌استه بود' places him 'in the khan (hall) that has/holds elephants'—پیل‌استه is a compound adjective 'elephant‑stood/elephant‑fitted' describing the khan. بود is the past copula 'was.'
segments.s494-g8: همه گرد بر گرد او رسته بود is a statement of disposition: 'everyone' (همه) 'around' (گرد) 'on/about' (بر گرد) 'him' (او) 'was drawn up' (رسته بود). رسته بود conveys that people/forces were formed/ranged in ranks around him; the doubled گرد/بر گرد is a poetic repetition emphasizing encirclement.
segments.s494-g17: 'چنان کن' is an imperative ('do so'). 'که' introduces a subordinate/purpose clause ('so that'). 'از پیش تو' literally 'from before you' = 'in your presence' (preposition + pronoun). 'بیطقون' here is a proper name/epithet placed as the logical subject of the subordinate clause.
segments.s494-g18: 'شود' (from شدن) is used as 'become' / jussive potential here ('may become' / 'become'). 'شاد و خشنود' are coordinated predicative adjectives 'happy and pleased'. 'با رهنمون' = 'with/by (your) guidance' where 'با' marks accompaniment and 'رهنمون' is a noun 'guidance'.
segments.s494-g19: 'به ره بر کسی' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'on/along the way toward anyone' (به + ره + بر + کسی). 'تا' here introduces purpose/limitation ('so that' / 'lest'), and 'نیازاردش' is a verb with the enclitic object '-ش' ('him') meaning 'cause him need/distress' (the lexical sense is 'make needy/harass').
segments.s494-g20: 'ور' is a conditional/ concessive particle ('if' / 'even if'). 'از دشمنان نیز' emphasizes 'even from (the) enemies' ('از' = from, 'نیز' = also/even). 'نشماردش' is the negative form of 'شمردن' with enclitic '-ش' and means 'do not count/consider him'; here it expresses the wished-for result in the hypothetical clause.
segments.s494-g21: که introduces a relative/clause-head meaning “who/that.” زنده کن is a compound in which کن is not an imperative here but the formative/agentive element (-کن), so زنده‌کن = “life‑giver / the one who makes (me) alive.” پاک جان من is a noun phrase with adjective before noun (“my pure life/soul”), where من is the possessor. اوست is the older copula (او + ست) meaning “he/it is,” completing the clause: “the one who keeps me alive is my pure soul.”
segments.s494-g22: برآنم is a fixed predicate meaning “I am resolved / bent on.” که introduces the subordinate clause that follows (“that …”). روشن‌روان is a compound noun (“bright‑soul,” i.e. clear‑souled or radiant of spirit) and من is again the possessor (‘my’). اوست is the copula “he/it is.” The whole second clause reads literally “I am resolved that he is my bright‑soul.”
segments.s494-g23: بدو is a prepositional/dative phrase “to him” (unto him) placed before the reporting verb. گفت here functions as the verb “said” (classical word order: indirect object + verb + subject), although the provided senses list did not include the verbal sense. که introduces the reported interrogative/indirect question “what …” or “how …”; ایدون is an archaic adverb/phrase here meaning roughly “now / at this time” (the sense list given doesn’t fit exactly). کنم is the 1st‑person subjunctive/optative “shall I do / may I do.” The segment therefore expresses “the mother said to him, ‘What shall I do now…?’”
segments.s494-g24: که again introduces the subordinate/purpose clause “that / so that.” او را is the object/emphatic direct object form (“him” or “his” with topical marking); را is the definite/object marker. بزرگی is the noun “greatness.” بر افزون کن(م) is a verbal construction meaning “to add to / to increase” (بر + افزون + کنم), with کنم the 1st‑person subjunctive “I may/must increase.” The whole clause means “that I may increase his greatness.”

sentences.s494-s17: May no country, crown, or throne remain to him — neither strength nor kingship, neither treasure nor fortune.
sentences.s494-s5: On the house's crown were figures of jasper and gold; within (were) several kinds of jewels set in gold.
sentences.s494-s6: Before the inner chamber was a bundle of musk-scent; two fitting youths stood before him.
sentences.s494-s7: When Tinush the horse‑breaker and Qeydrosh (appeared), they bent an ear to Qeydafeh’s words.
sentences.s494-s8: To the mother he thus spoke: “O lesser son — O king, auspicious star and just ruler.”
sentences.s494-s13: He said to Alexander, the renowned king: "Make what is hidden manifest now."
sentences.s494-s14: What do you want, and what is Alexander's counsel about it? O Rani, what is your relation to the king, and whose command is this?
sentences.s494-s15: Alexander said to him, 'O exalted one, my stay in your presence has been long.'
sentences.s494-s16: He said to me, 'Go and demand his frontier; and if you tarry, I will bring an army.'
sentences.s494-s1: Alexander came, his heart like a mountain, freed by the scholarly king.
sentences.s494-s2: There was no trace of the Chinese chain on his brow; his heart never once gave up its longing.
sentences.s494-s3: That night and at early dawn he came from the iwan to the king's presence.
sentences.s494-s4: The commander stood in the elephant‑stabled khan; everyone was drawn up around him.
sentences.s494-s9: Do this so that Biṭaqūn, in your presence, may become happy and content by your guidance.
sentences.s494-s10: See that on the road no one causes him hardship; and even if it is from enemies, do not count him (i.e. do not treat him as an enemy).
sentences.s494-s11: The one who keeps me alive is my pure soul; I am resolved that he is my bright‑soul.
sentences.s494-s12: The mother said to him, “What shall I do now so that I may increase his greatness?”
